content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 7, 2018

Tom Schroeter
City Attorney for City of Tehachapi
254 H Street
Bakersfield, CA 93304

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-058

Dear Mr. Schroeter:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge take part in a decision to approve an airport hangar use and inspection policy even though the councilmember leases airport property from the City where he owns and operates his business?

CONCLUSION

No. The councilmember may not take part in the City Council's decision regarding an airport hangar use and inspection policy.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

The City of Tehachapi (the "City") owns and operates Tehachapi Municipal Airport (the "Airport"). City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge has several ground leases with the City for the use

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-058
Page No. 2

of Airport property on which hangars have been constructed. Councilmember Hetge operates his
business out of the hangars. His business operations consist primarily of performing aircraft
maintenance (repairs, modifications, and inspections), but also include aircraft storage, aircraft
hangar rental, and aircraft rental. Councilmember Hetge has operated his businesses at the Airport
for over ten years. He owns his business in partnership with his wife, as sole proprietors, with no
other employees.

Councilmember Hetge indicated that none of his leases with the City will expire during his
present term on the City Council, nor will any of his leases expire during the four years following
his current term, should he seek reelection.

The City Council will be considering and voting on the City of Tehachapi Hangar Use and
Inspection Policy (the "Hangar Use Policy"). The Hangar Use Policy governs permissive uses and
business practices for the entire Airport property, including areas leased by private citizens.
Councilmember Hetge indicated that his ground lease agreements with the City essentially,
duplicate the language from the Hangar Use Policy.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Pertinent to the provided facts, the following interests
are relevant to your request for advice:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b)).²

• An interest in any source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An
official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.³

___________________________

²"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, except periodic tenancies of one month or less. (Section
82033; Regulation 18233.)

³While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family
member (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(c)(6)), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered
separately where the decision affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).)

File No. A-18-058
Page No. 3

Under the provided facts, Councilmember Hetge has a business interest in the business he
owns in partnership with his wife. He also has interests in the business and customers of the
business as sources of income if he receives $500 or more in income from the business or the
customers of the business within 12 months prior to the decision. Additionally, Councilmember
Hetge and his wife have a property interest in his leased property.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) 4 For a financial interest
involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Business Entity and Source of Income Interests

The materiality standard for interests in business entities, including business entities that are
sources of income to the official, is provided in Regulation 18702.1. (See also Regulation 18702.3.)
As pertinent to the facts provided, Regulation 18702.1(a)(6) provides that a decision's effect on an
official's interest in a business entity is material if the business entity is the subject of an
inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency.

The Hangar Use Policy governs permissive uses and business practices, including an
inspection policy, for those businesses operating on Airport property. Because the policy is limited
to only those businesses operating at the airport, these businesses are the subject of the City Council's
decision to adopt the Hangar Use Policy, which is an inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the
City's regulatory authority. Accordingly, the councilmember's business and any business that leases
a hangar from the councilmember are explicitly involved in the decisions and the effect of the
decisions on the business or business is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and material under
Regulation 18702.1(a)(6).

Notwithstanding the determination that the effect of the decisions is material under
Regulation 18702.1(a)(6), we note that Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the financial effect of a
governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant." Because
Councilmember Hetge's lease agreements with the City essentially duplicate the language found in
the Hangar Use Policy, we must further consider whether the Hangar Use Policy's effect on the
councilmember's interests is "nominal, inconsequential, and insignificant."

In this case, although the Hangar Use Policy does not impose new restrictions on the
Councilmember's business or operations, the Hangar Use Policy does potentially impose
restrictions on other competing businesses operating at the Airport. Accordingly, it does appear that

4 A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes
any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).
(Regulation 18701(a).)

File No. A-18-058
Page No. 4

the effect on Councilmember Hetge's business is more than nominal, inconsequential, or
insignificant. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Hetge is disqualified from the
decision.⁵

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

⁵ In light of this conclusion, we do not analysis the potential effect on Councilmember Hetge's property
interest in his leases or interests in customers of his business as sources of income.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public 
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Pertinent to the provided facts, the following interests 
are relevant to your request for advice: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, · 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect'interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).)2 
• 
An interest in any source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An 
official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.3 
2 "Interest in real property'' includes any leasehold, except periodic tenancies of one month or less. (Section 
82033; Regulation 18233.) 
3 While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family 
member (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(c)(6)), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered 
separately where the decision affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.S(c).)  
Under the provided facts, Councilmember Hetge has a business interest in the business he 
owns in partnership with his wife. He also has interests in the business and customers of the 
business as sources of income ifhe receives $500 or more in income from the business or the 
customers of the business within 12 months prior to the decision. Additionally, Councilmember

decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701 (a).)4 For a financial interest 
that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870 I (b ). )

The materiality standard for interests in business entities, including business entities that are 
sources of income to the official, is provided in Regulation 18702.1. (See also Regulation 18702.3.) 
As pertinent to the facts provided, Regulation 18702 .1 ( a)( 6) provides that a decision's effect on an

inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency. 
The Hangar Use Policy governs permissive uses and business practices, including an

to only those businesses operating at the airport, these businesses are the subject the City Council's 
decision to adopt the Hangar Use Policy, which is an inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the 
City's regulatory authority. Accordingly, the councilmember's business and any business that leases 
a hanger from the councilmember are explicitly involved in the decisions and the effect of the 
decisions on the business or business is foreseeable under Regulation 1870 l (a) and material under 
Regulation 18702.l(a)(6). 
Notwithstanding the determination that the effect of the decisions is material under 
Regulation 18702.l(a)(6), we note that Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the financial effect of a 
governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant." Because 
Councilmember Hetge's lease agreements with the City essentially duplicate the language found in 
the Hangar Use Policy, we must further consider whether the Hangar Use Policy's effect on the 
Councilmember's interests is "nominal, inconsequential, and insignificant." 
In this case, although the Hangar Use Policy does not impose new restrictions on the 
Councilmember's business or operations, the Hangar Use Policy does potentially impose 
restrictions on other competing businesses operating at the Airport. Accordingly, it does appear that 
4 A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes 
any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
(Regulation 1870 l(a).)  
the effect on Councilmember Hetge's business is more than nominal, inconsequential, or 
insignificant. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Hetge is disqualified from the 
decision.5
